The line integral of the function $ f(z) = e^{z^2} $ along the boundary of the circle $ |z| = R $ can be evaluated using contour integration. Since $ f(z) = e^{z^2} $ is an entire function (analytic everywhere in the complex plane), the integral over any closed contour is equal to the integral over the entire complex plane. However, since the function is not analytic in the interior of the circle, we cannot directly apply the Cauchy integral theorem. Instead, we consider the integral over the circle and use the residue theorem, which is not applicable here due to the function being entire. Therefore, the integral of $ f(z) = e^{z^2} $ around the circle $ |z| = R $ is equal to the integral of $ e^{z^2} $ over the entire complex plane, which is not zero. However, the integral of $ e^{z^2} $ over a closed contour is not zero because the function is not analytic in the interior of the circle. Thus, the line integral is equal to the integral of $ e^{z^2} $ over the entire complex plane, which is not zero. The value of the integral is not zero due to the non-analytic nature of the function in the interior of the circle.

The average energy density over the entire area of the circle can be calculated by integrating the real part of $ f(z) = e^{z^2} $ over the area of the circle and dividing by the area of the circle. In polar coordinates, $ z = r e^{i\theta} $, and $ e^{z^2} = e^{r^2 e^{i2\theta}} = e^{r^2 (\cos 2\theta + i \sin 2\theta)} $. The real part of $ e^{z^2} $ is $ e^{r^2 \cos 2\theta} $. The average energy density is the integral of $ e^{r^2 \cos 2\theta} $ over the area of the circle divided by the area of the circle. This integral is not zero because the function $ e^{r^2 \cos 2\theta} $ is not symmetric in a way that would make the average zero.
